adams_view_validation.description: For any eval that produces .cmd file output, run it through Adams View to check for errors. This is a programmatic check — do not rely on the LLM to do this.
adams_view_validation.pass_condition: Exit code 0 (no ERROR: lines in aview.log)
notes: The script locates mdi.bat automatically (VS Code setting → ADAMS_LAUNCH_COMMAND → ADAMS_INSTALL_DIR → default path)
Adams View runs in the .cmd file's parent directory and produces aview.log there
The --log-output flag copies aview.log to the eval output directory for review
The aview.log should also be saved as an output file alongside the .cmd for the reviewer to inspect

evals.prompt: Write an Adams CMD script to create a simple pendulum. It should have a single rigid link of length 200 mm and mass 1 kg, pinned to the ground at its top end by a revolute joint, with gravity acting in the -Y direction. Release it from 45 degrees.

rubric.model_created: Script creates a model with model create command
rubric.part_created: A rigid body part is created with correct syntax
rubric.markers_created: At least a pin marker is created on the link and ground
rubric.revolute_joint: A revolute joint is created connecting the link to ground using i_marker_name and j_marker_name
rubric.gravity: Gravity field is created with -Y component (e.g. -9806.65 or -9.80665)
rubric.initial_angle: Part or marker orientation set to 45 degrees for initial release
rubric.full_keyword_names: All keywords spelled out fully (not abbreviated)
rubric.no_adams_id: No adams_id specified anywhere in the script
rubric.mass_properties_uses_modify: Mass properties set with part modify (not part create) since the part already exists
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape (sphere, cylinder, or box) is added to each moving part for visual inspection in Adams View
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View without any ERROR: lines in aview.log (validated by scripts/run_adams_cmd.py)

evals.prompt: How do I create a spring-damper force between two parts in Adams CMD? The spring should have stiffness 5000 N/mm and damping 100 N·s/mm, with a free length of 250 mm.

rubric.correct_command: Uses force create element_like translational_spring_damper (not single_component_force or bushing)
rubric.explains_when_to_use: Explains that spring_damper is for 1-DOF along a line of action

evals.prompt: Write a FUNCTION= expression for a one-sided contact force that activates when a part's CM falls below Z = 0 (i.e., hits a floor at Z=0). The contact should have stiffness 1e5, exponent 1.5, damping 50, and penetration depth 0.1 mm.

rubric.uses_impact: Uses the IMPACT() function (not BISTOP or a manual IF expression)
rubric.correct_displacement_arg: First arg uses DZ or similar displacement measuring penetration below floor
rubric.correct_velocity_arg: Second arg uses VZ or matching velocity measure
rubric.threshold_at_zero: Threshold value is 0.0 (or equivalent contact point)
rubric.correct_params: Stiffness 1e5, exponent 1.5, damping 50, penetration 0.1 all present
rubric.quoted_expression: Expression wrapped in double quotes as required for FUNCTION= parameter

evals.prompt: Write an Adams CMD script to build a four-bar linkage mechanism. It has three moving rigid links: a crank (100 mm long, 0.5 kg), a coupler (250 mm long, 0.5 kg), and a rocker (200 mm long, 0.5 kg). The crank is pinned to the ground at the origin; the rocker is pinned to the ground 300 mm to the right. Connect the free ends of the crank and rocker with the coupler via revolute joints. Drive the crank at a constant 360 degrees per second. Simulate for 2 seconds at 0.001 s steps.

rubric.model_created: Script creates a model with model create command
rubric.three_moving_parts: Three distinct rigid body parts are created (crank, coupler, rocker)
rubric.four_revolute_joints: Four revolute joints are created (crank-ground, crank-coupler, coupler-rocker, rocker-ground)
rubric.motion_generator_on_crank: A motion generator drives the crank joint
rubric.constant_speed_360: Motion generator function specifies 360 degrees per second (e.g. 360D * TIME or equivalent)
rubric.simulate_transient: Script uses simulation single_run transient command (or notes the command to run)
rubric.full_keyword_names: All keywords spelled out fully — no abbreviated commands anywhere
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy (.model.part.marker)
rubric.no_adams_id: No adams_id specified anywhere in the script
rubric.mass_properties_uses_modify: Mass/inertia set with part modify rigid_body mass_properties, not part create
rubric.creates_cm_and_passes_center_of_mass_mar: A .cm marker is explicitly created on each part BEFORE part modify mass_properties is called, and center_of_mass_marker is passed in every part modify rigid_body mass_properties call
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape (sphere, cylinder, or box) is added to each moving part (crank, coupler, rocker) for visual inspection
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View (via scripts/run_adams_cmd.py) without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt: I need to model a rubber mount between a chassis and a subframe. The X-direction (translational) stiffness is nonlinear and follows this force-displacement table: at −10 mm displacement, −8000 N; at −5 mm, −3000 N; at 0 mm, 0 N; at 5 mm, 3000 N; at 10 mm, 8000 N. The Y and Z translational stiffness is linear at 5000 N/mm. Rotational stiffness is 200 N·mm/deg about all three axes. Write an Adams CMD script that creates both the chassis (fixed to ground) and subframe as rigid parts (10 kg each, Ixx=Iyy=Izz=1000 kg·mm² each), adds appropriate markers, and implements this elastic joint correctly.

rubric.spline_data_element_created: A data_element create spline command defines the X stiffness table
rubric.correct_spline_x_points: Spline x values include -10, -5, 0, 5, 10 (or equivalent in correct units)
rubric.general_force_used: force create direct general_force is used for the X-direction force (required to implement AKISPL nonlinear curve; a simple bushing only accepts constant stiffness per DOF)
rubric.akispl_in_x_force_function: x_force_function uses AKISPL() to look up force from the spline based on DX displacement
rubric.yz_force_functions_linear: Y and Z translational stiffness of 5000 N/mm implemented — either as y/z force functions in the GFORCE ('-5000 * DY(...)'), or via a separate bushing element with Ky=Kz=5000 N/mm
rubric.rotational_functions_200: Rotational stiffness of 200 N·mm/deg implemented. Preferred approach: a separate bushing element with angular stiffness converted to N·mm/rad (200 × π/180 ≈ 3.491 N·mm/rad per axis, i.e. bushing stiffness = 0.0, 5000.0, 5000.0, 3.491, 3.491, 3.491). Using AX/AY/AZ angle sensors in GFORCE torque functions also works but can wrap at large angles. Do NOT use the invalid 'rotational_stiffness' bushing parameter.
rubric.mass_properties_uses_modify: Mass/inertia set with part modify rigid_body mass_properties, not part create
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy
rubric.no_adams_id: No adams_id specified anywhere in the script
rubric.creates_cm_and_passes_center_of_mass_mar: A .cm marker is explicitly created on each rigid part BEFORE part modify mass_properties is called, and center_of_mass_marker is passed in every part modify rigid_body mass_properties call
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape is added to each moving part for visual inspection
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View (via scripts/run_adams_cmd.py) without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt: Write a complete Adams CMD script for a cam-follower mechanism. Create a cam body (1 kg, Ixx=Iyy=Izz=100 kg·mm²) fixed to ground at the origin and a follower (0.5 kg rigid body, Ixx=Iyy=50 kg·mm², Izz=10 kg·mm²) that can only translate along the global X axis via a translational joint. Apply a single-component force using STEP5 that pushes the follower in the +X direction: the force should ramp smoothly from 0 N to 500 N as the follower's DX displacement (from .model.follower.tip_mkr relative to .model.cam.ref_mkr) goes from 0 mm to 20 mm, then hold at 500 N beyond 20 mm. Add a velocity-dependent damping of 2.0 × VX. Add simple geometry shapes (cylinder or box) to both the cam and follower parts. Simulate for 1 second.

rubric.model_and_parts_created: Script creates a model with at least two parts: a cam (fixed to ground) and a follower
rubric.translational_joint: A translational joint constrains the follower to translate along X
rubric.uses_step5_not_step: STEP5 is used for the cam force (not plain STEP — STEP5 has continuous 2nd derivative)
rubric.correct_dx_range_0_to_20: STEP5 x-range spans 0 to 20 (follower displacement from 0 to 20 mm)
rubric.correct_force_range_0_to_500: STEP5 output range spans 0 to 500 (force from 0 to 500 N)
rubric.hold_beyond_20mm: Force holds at 500 N for DX > 20 mm (STEP5 naturally saturates at h1)
rubric.velocity_damping_2_vx: Damping term + 2.0 * VX(...) added to the force expression
rubric.quoted_expression: The FUNCTION= value is expressed as one or more double-quoted strings (comma-separated quoted strings are valid Adams CMD syntax for splitting long expressions and should be marked TRUE)
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape is added to each part (cam and follower) for visual inspection
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy
rubric.simulate_transient: Script uses simulation single_run transient command
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt: Write an Adams CMD script that creates a chain of 8 point masses in a straight line along the X axis, spaced 50 mm apart, starting at the origin. The first mass is fixed to ground with a fixed joint. Each adjacent pair of masses is connected by a spring-damper with stiffness 100 N/mm, damping coefficient 1.0 N·s/mm, and free length 50 mm. Each mass is 0.25 kg. Use a FOR loop with parametric string construction (EVAL, RTOI, //) to create the masses and spring-dampers rather than writing them out individually. Simulate for 2 seconds.

rubric.for_loop_used: A for / end loop drives mass and/or spring-damper creation
rubric.eval_function_used: EVAL() used to dereference a variable inside a command string
rubric.rtoi_function_used: RTOI() used to convert the loop counter to a string for name construction
rubric.string_concat_operator_used: // string concatenation operator used to build part/marker names
rubric.point_mass_type_used: Masses created with part create point_mass (not rigid_body)
rubric.spring_damper_created: Spring-dampers created with force create element_like translational_spring_damper with stiffness=100, damping=1.0, displacement_at_preload=50
rubric.first_mass_fixed_to_ground: First mass (mass_1) is fixed to ground with a spherical joint or atpoint primitive (NOT fixed joint)
rubric.50mm_spacing: Masses are spaced 50 mm apart along the X axis
rubric.simulate_transient: Script uses simulation single_run transient command (or notes the command to run)
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy
rubric.no_adams_id: No adams_id specified anywhere in the script
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape (sphere or cylinder) is added to each mass for visual inspection
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View (via scripts/run_adams_cmd.py) without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt: Write a complete Adams CMD script for a two-shaft gear system. Create two shaft parts (each 0.5 kg, Ixx=Iyy=1000 kg·mm², Izz=50 kg·mm²) connected to ground via revolute joints on the Z axis — the input shaft joint at the origin, the output shaft joint at 0, 150, 0 mm. Link the two revolute joints (.model.rev_input and .model.rev_output) with a gear coupler at a 3:1 speed ratio where the input rotates 3× faster than the output and they rotate in opposite directions. Add a motion generator on the input joint that smoothly ramps the input shaft angular velocity from 0 to 120 degrees per second over the first 0.5 seconds using the haversine step function (HAVSIN), then holds at 120 deg/s afterward. Add cylinder geometry to both shafts. Simulate for 2 seconds.

rubric.model_and_parts_created: Script creates a model with at least two shaft parts connected to ground via revolute joints
rubric.coupler_constraint_used: constraint create complex_joint coupler is used (NOT 'constraint create coupler' — the full sub-command is 'complex_joint')
rubric.motion_multipliers_correct: motion_multipliers = 1.0, -0.333 (or equivalent 3:1 ratio with opposite sign for opposite rotation direction)
rubric.both_joints_referenced: joint_name lists both .model.rev_input and .model.rev_output in the coupler
rubric.motion_generator_created: constraint create motion_generator is used on the input joint
rubric.time_derivative_velocity: motion generator specifies time_derivative = velocity — required so Adams interprets the HAVSIN output as angular velocity, not position
rubric.uses_havsin_not_step: HAVSIN function is used for the ramp (not STEP or STEP5)
rubric.correct_havsin_argument_order: HAVSIN argument order is (x, x0, x1, h0, h1) = (TIME, 0.0, 0.5, 0.0, 120D)
rubric.converts_to_radians_or_uses_D: 120 degrees/s expressed correctly — either as 120D for degrees or converted to rad/s
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry shape (cylinder or similar) is added to each shaft part
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy
rubric.simulate_transient: Script uses simulation single_run transient command
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt:
This Adams CMD script has several bugs. Find and fix all of them. Your output should be a complete, corrected script that runs without errors in Adams View.

```
model cre model_name = .pendulum

defaults units length = mm mass = kg

marker create &
    marker_name = .pendulum.ground.pivot_j &
    location    = 0.0, 0.0, 0.0

marker create &
    marker_name = .pendulum.ground.wall_j &
    location    = 0.0, -200.0, 0.0

part cre rig nam &
    part_name = .pendulum.link &
    location  = 0.0, 0.0, 0.0

part create rigid_body mass_properties &
    part_name             = .pendulum.link &
    adams_id              = 3 &
    mass                  = 1.0 &
    center_of_mass_marker = .pendulum.link.cm &
    ixx                   = 3333.0 &
    iyy                   = 3333.0 &
    izz                   = 100.0

marker create &
    marker_name = pendulum.link.pin_mkr &
    location    = 0.0, 0.0, 0.0

marker create &
    marker_name = .pendulum.link.tip_mkr &
    location    = 0.0, -200.0, 0.0

constraint create joint revolute &
    joint_name    = .pendulum.rev_pin &
    i_marker_name = pendulum.link.pin_mkr &
    j_marker_name = .pendulum.ground.pivot_j

force create body gravitational &
    gravity_field_name  = .pendulum.gravity &
    x_component_gravity = 0.0 &
    y_component_gravity = -9806.65 &
    z_component_gravity = 0.0

force create element_like spring_damper &
    spring_damper_name = .pendulum.return_spring &
    i_marker_name      = .pendulum.link.tip_mkr &
    j_marker_name      = .pendulum.ground.wall_j &
    stiffness          = 10.0 &
    damping            = 0.1 &
    length             = 200.0

sim trans end_time = 2.0 step_size = 0.001
```

rubric.fixes_model_create_abbreviation: Identifies 'model cre' as an abbreviated keyword (bad practice in scripts, though technically valid in Adams) and expands to 'model create'
rubric.fixes_part_create_abbreviation: Identifies 'part cre rig nam' as abbreviated and expands to 'part create rigid_body name_and_position' (best-practice fix)
rubric.changes_mass_properties_create_to_modify: Changes 'part create rigid_body mass_properties' to 'part modify rigid_body mass_properties' — the part already exists from the name_and_position call, so 'create' is a real error here
rubric.removes_adams_id: Removes 'adams_id = 3' — while technically valid in Adams, manually assigning IDs is unnecessary and error-prone
rubric.keeps_center_of_mass_marker_cm_created_f: The .pendulum.link.cm marker is explicitly created BEFORE the part modify mass_properties call, and center_of_mass_marker = .pendulum.link.cm is kept in that call. The bug in the original script is that .cm was referenced before it existed — the fix is to create it first, not remove it.
rubric.fixes_missing_dot_paths: Corrects 'pendulum.link.pin_mkr' to '.pendulum.link.pin_mkr' (missing leading dot, appears in both marker create and i_marker_name)
rubric.fixes_spring_damper_keyword: Changes 'force create element_like spring_damper' to 'force create element_like translational_spring_damper' and 'length = 200.0' to 'displacement_at_preload = 200.0'
rubric.fixes_simulation_command: Corrects 'sim trans end_time = 2.0 step_size = 0.001' to 'simulation single_run transient & type = auto_select & end_time = 2.0 & number_of_steps = 2000 & model_name = .pendulum & initial_static = no'
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: The corrected CMD script runs in Adams View without any ERROR: lines in aview.log

evals.prompt:
Write an Adams CMD script for a model called 'crane'. It has three rigid parts:

1. A tower (5 kg, Ixx=Iyy=10000 kg·mm², Izz=500 kg·mm²) at 0, 0, 500 mm
2. A boom (3 kg, Ixx=Iyy=8000 kg·mm², Izz=300 kg·mm²) at 400, 0, 1000 mm
3. A payload (10 kg, Ixx=Iyy=Izz=100 kg·mm²) at 800, 0, 800 mm

On the boom, create a marker called mkr_boom_tip using eval with loc_global at local offset {200, 0, 0} from the boom's CM. Orient it using eval with ori_along_axis so its z-axis points from the boom's CM marker (.crane.boom.cm) toward the payload's CM marker (.crane.payload.cm) — use the full dot-path names in the ori_along_axis call.

On the payload, create a marker called mkr_payload_hang. Place it using eval with loc_relative_to that computes a position offset {0, 0, 150} relative to the boom's tip marker (.crane.boom.mkr_boom_tip) expressed in the ground frame (.crane.ground.origin). Orient it using eval with ori_along_axis so its z-axis points from .crane.boom.mkr_boom_tip toward .crane.payload.cm — again use full dot-path names.

Connect tower to ground with a revolute joint at the tower base, boom to tower top with a revolute joint, and payload to boom tip with a spherical joint.

Apply a single-component force on the payload with this FUNCTION= expression: STEP5(TIME, 0.0, 0.0, 0.5, -500.0) + STEP5(TIME, 1.0, 0.0, 1.5, 500.0) - 2.0 * VZ(.crane.payload.cm, .crane.ground.origin, .crane.ground.origin, .crane.ground.origin) + IMPACT(DZ(.crane.payload.cm, .crane.ground.origin, .crane.ground.origin), VZ(.crane.payload.cm, .crane.ground.origin, .crane.ground.origin, .crane.ground.origin), 0.0, 1.0E5, 1.5, 50.0, 0.1)

Add cylinder geometry to the tower and boom, and an ellipsoid to the payload. Simulate for 3 seconds with 3000 steps.

Try to keep lines under 80 characters where the CMD syntax allows it.

rubric.model_and_parts_created: Script creates a model named 'crane' with three rigid body parts (tower, boom, payload)
rubric.markers_use_eval_loc: Boom and payload markers use eval() with loc_global() or loc_relative_to() to compute positions
rubric.markers_use_ori_along_axis: Both mkr_boom_tip and mkr_payload_hang use eval with ori_along_axis() with full dot-path marker names in the arguments
rubric.eval_expressions_single_line: Every eval expression — anything inside parentheses like (eval(...)) — stays entirely on one line, no matter how long it gets. The ori_along_axis calls with full dot-path names like .crane.boom.cm and .crane.payload.cm produce long lines, but they must NOT be split with &. This is the critical check.
rubric.function_string_wrapped: The long FUNCTION= expression (STEP5 + STEP5 + VZ + IMPACT) is split across multiple lines using comma-separated quoted strings. It should NOT be crammed onto one enormous line — the syntax allows splitting for function strings.
rubric.joints_correct: Two revolute joints (tower-ground, boom-tower) and one spherical joint (payload-boom) are created
rubric.force_expression_correct: The force function includes STEP5 ramps, a VZ velocity-damping term, and an IMPACT contact term
rubric.mass_properties_uses_modify: Mass/inertia set with part modify rigid_body mass_properties
rubric.dot_path_names: All object names use full dot-path hierarchy
rubric.geometry_present: Cylinder geometry on tower and boom, ellipsoid on payload
rubric.simulate_transient: Script uses simulation single_run transient for 3 seconds with 3000 steps
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: CMD script runs in Adams View without any ERROR: lines in aview.log
